title: Occult Artifacts & Objects
description: Objects with occult properties — things that are worth acquiring, worth avoiding, and worth understanding before you put them down.

entries.result: A watch that runs backward at the correct speed when held by someone with unfinished business. The amount of time left on a particular obligation can be read from it.
A compact mirror that, when closed and opened in a specific place, shows the room as it will appear in twenty-four hours. The image lasts eleven seconds.
A pen that writes what the holder intends rather than what they write. Requires significant concentration to use for anything other than one's actual thoughts.
A set of cards that, when laid out and read correctly, shows the relationships between people in the room. Does not show content of those relationships — only that they exist, and their strength.
A flask whose contents cannot be positively identified until consumed, at which point the contents become clear to the person who drank them. The contents change each time the flask is refilled.
A coin that always returns to the pocket of whoever was most recently deceived in its presence.
A key that fits any lock it is brought to, but opens each lock only once and then no longer fits it.
A photograph of a place that updates in real time, showing the location as it currently appears. The location is fixed. The photographer is unknown.
A letter opener that can be used to unseal any sealed document without physical damage. Documents unsealed with it can subsequently be physically resealed without visible evidence of opening.
A pair of gloves that prevent anything the wearer touches from retaining their fingerprints, impressions, or traces. The gloves themselves retain everything.
A notebook in which written names occasionally reorder themselves overnight. The order appears to be meaningful but the key to the system has not been found.
A cigarette case that, when opened, allows the holder to appear completely unremarkable — not invisible, simply unnoticed — for the duration of a single cigarette.
A ring that becomes warm when the wearer is being directly lied to. Becomes cold when the truth being told is incomplete. Neutral on omissions.
A glass paperweight containing what appears to be a city. The city is not any city the crew recognizes. It changes when not observed.
A small brass figure that finds the nearest source of a specific thing — water, danger, a specific person — when placed on a flat surface and spun.
A length of cord that, when tied and untied in specific patterns, can bind or loose obligations between parties who agree to it. Both parties must hold it simultaneously.
A stamp that marks documents with an authority that no entity can currently identify but all appear to respect.
A small bottle of ink that, when used to write on skin, makes the writing invisible to everyone except the writer and the subject.
An address book in a dead person's handwriting that continues to update with new entries. The dead person's contact network, it appears, has not been informed of their death.
A revolver with one round that will not fire until the correct moment. The hammer has been cocked twice. Both times, something intervened before it discharged. Something is waiting for the third.
A clock that shows two times simultaneously — the correct local time and a time in another location that the clock chooses. The second location changes.
A small box that cannot be opened by the person who currently owns it. The previous owner couldn't open it either. There is something inside.
A record that, when played, broadcasts a different sound in each room through which it is audible. The sounds are consistent across multiple plays.
A set of spectacles that, when worn, allow the wearer to read any written text regardless of language. The translation is literal and sometimes unhelpful.
A telephone that can connect to any number, including numbers that do not exist in the current telephone directory. The caller must know what they're calling.

A coat that changes its apparent quality based on the expectations of the person looking at it. A person who expects to see a rich person sees fine fabric. A person who expects to see a vagrant sees rags.
A hat that, when worn, makes the wearer's voice authoritative to whoever is in front of them. Not persuasive — authoritative. People comply without knowing why.
A briefcase with an interior that is significantly larger than its exterior. Reliable. Has been used for years. No one knows what the original purpose was.
A candle that, when lit in the presence of a binding or agreement, illuminates it visually — contracts appear as burning text, oaths as traces of light between the parties.
A map of the city that shows a second city overlaid on it, invisible except when the map is held at a specific angle in specific light. The second city exists.
